target.proposedChange: Implemented draggable flowchart entities and distinct relationship edge/label colors with a legend, while preserving static fallback.

relationshipTypes.BRANCHES_ON: The source uses the value in control flow.
relationshipTypes.TRANSFORMS_TO: The value contributes to a derived value.
relationshipTypes.EXPOSES: The source presents the value to the UI.

commits.subject: chore: establish project baseline
commits.reason: Initial tracked baseline containing the complete current viewer interaction and rendering implementation.

evidence.detail: Adds the viewer, renderer dependency, and usage documentation as the repository baseline.

commits.subject: feat(viewer): add interactive flowchart entities
commits.reason: Introduces native flowchart parsing, draggable entity position mutation, relationship color mapping, legend rendering, tests, and documented fallback behavior.

evidence.detail: Adds InteractiveGraph and FlowchartParser, renders native canvas entities/edges, updates positions during drag, and adds relationship color tests.

limitations: Repository history begins at the imported baseline; pre-initialization history is unavailable.

The bundled history script was run with a process-local execution-policy bypass; its comma-separated arguments were interpreted as combined patterns, so confirmed-path and manual diff review supplied the post-change evidence.

changeScenarios.subject: value:mermaid-viewer.graph-interaction-state
compatibilityRisks: Only supported flowchart syntax uses the native interactive path; unknown syntax deliberately falls back to Mermaid CLI.
More than eight unique relation labels reuse palette colors.
requiredVerification: run unit tests with Python installed
drag each entity and verify connected edges/labels move
verify legend colors match edges and labels
verify zoom, pan, fit, reload, and static fallback
changeScenarios.rollout: Desktop application update with automatic per-file fallback

questionsAnswered.question: What is the exact entity and value being mapped?
questionsAnswered.answer: MermaidViewer.graphInteractionState, now including InteractiveGraph.positions, drag_node_id, and relation_colors.

questionsAnswered.question: What does the value mean in the business or application domain?
questionsAnswered.answer: It controls whether a Mermaid file is displayed as a draggable native flowchart or a static fallback and how entities, relations, zoom, and pan behave.

questionsAnswered.question: What are its type, allowed values, nullability, default, constraints, and invariants?
questionsAnswered.answer: Composite runtime state: graph and images are nullable; positions map node IDs to float coordinate pairs; drag_node_id is nullable; zoom remains 0.1..4.0; relation colors map labels to an eight-color palette.

questionsAnswered.question: What aliases represent the same value in code, APIs, storage, events, UI, configuration, and documentation?
questionsAnswered.answer: Aliases now include graph, graph.positions, drag_node_id, relation_colors, canvas graph/node/edge tags, plus the prior source/image/zoom aliases.

questionsAnswered.question: Where does the value originate?
questionsAnswered.answer: Flowchart state originates from UTF-8 .mmd content parsed by FlowchartParser; positions originate from _layout; colors originate from first-seen relationship labels.

questionsAnswered.question: Which constructors, defaults, APIs, imports, events, configuration sources, database reads, or external systems can supply it?
questionsAnswered.answer: The file dialog and local .mmd file supply source; FlowchartParser supplies nodes, edges, and positions; Mermaid CLI supplies fallback PNG output.

questionsAnswered.question: Which methods, functions, jobs, handlers, queries, or users can create, assign, or mutate it?
questionsAnswered.answer: FlowchartParser creates graph state; _show_interactive_graph assigns it; _drag_pan mutates node positions; zoom/fit mutate zoom; static render methods mutate image state.

questionsAnswered.question: Can any code bypass the intended mutation path?
questionsAnswered.answer: Python attributes remain publicly mutable, but no repository code bypasses the parser/drag/viewer mutation paths.
gaps: Dynamic external Python callers remain possible.

questionsAnswered.question: Where is the value validated, normalized, sanitized, authorized, or rejected?
questionsAnswered.answer: The parser validates the diagram header and supported syntax, rejects partial parsing, clamps zoom, and preserves static fallback; no authorization layer applies.

questionsAnswered.question: Where is the value compared, switched on, matched, filtered, or used to control a branch?
questionsAnswered.answer: Diagram type, parse success, graph/image presence, node tags, drag state, edge style, node shape, and Shift modifier control branches.

questionsAnswered.question: Where is the value stored, indexed, cached, copied, logged, measured, backed up, or searched?
questionsAnswered.answer: Interactive positions and colors exist only in memory; source remains a local file and fallback pixels remain temporary PNG files.
gaps: Dragged positions are not persisted across reloads.

questionsAnswered.question: Which database columns, schemas, constraints, migrations, and serialization formats represent it?
questionsAnswered.answer: No database exists. Input serialization is Mermaid text; fallback output is PNG; interactive graph state is Python dataclasses and dictionaries.

questionsAnswered.question: Which APIs, commands, forms, files, or messages accept it?
questionsAnswered.answer: The file dialog accepts .mmd files; FlowchartParser accepts source text; mmdc accepts unsupported diagrams through command arguments.

questionsAnswered.question: Which APIs, views, reports, exports, events, logs, or metrics expose it?
questionsAnswered.answer: The native canvas exposes entities and colored relations; the legend exposes label-to-color mapping; status exposes graph counts; unsupported formats expose a bitmap.

questionsAnswered.question: Which derived values are calculated from it?
questionsAnswered.answer: Derived values include layout positions, relationship color map, edge endpoints, fit zoom, screen positions, and scroll region.

questionsAnswered.question: Which methods, UI components, permissions, workflows, side effects, and external consumers depend on it directly?
questionsAnswered.answer: Parser, canvas entity renderer, drag handlers, legend, zoom/fit, background pan, status text, tests, and static fallback depend directly on graph state.

questionsAnswered.question: What depends on those direct consumers transitively?
questionsAnswered.answer: Text readability, connected-edge geometry, label ownership clarity, scrollbar extents, reload behavior, and README guidance depend transitively on those consumers.

questionsAnswered.question: Which caches, calculations, contracts, assumptions, or indexes must be invalidated or rebuilt when it changes?
questionsAnswered.answer: Every node move redraws edges, labels, and nodes; zoom/fit redraw the native graph; reload reconstructs positions and colors. No external cache/index exists.

questionsAnswered.question: Which tests specify or depend on its current behavior?
questionsAnswered.answer: test_interactive_graph.py specifies node/edge parsing, shapes, color stability/distinctness, non-flowchart rejection, and incomplete-syntax fallback.
gaps: Tests could not execute because no Python interpreter is installed in the environment.

questionsAnswered.question: Which commits introduced or changed the entity, value, aliases, constraints, mutations, checks, storage, APIs, events, and downstream behavior?
questionsAnswered.answer: The baseline commit introduced static state; c57f240 introduced interactive graph, position, drag, relation color, legend, tests, and fallback behavior.

questionsAnswered.question: Why is each included commit relevant, and what evidence supports that conclusion?
questionsAnswered.answer: Both commits were included after diff review: the first establishes the static baseline, while c57f240 adds all interactive state and consumers.

questionsAnswered.question: Is repository history complete, shallow, squashed, renamed, split across repositories, or otherwise limited?
questionsAnswered.answer: History is not shallow but begins at an imported baseline; pre-initialization history is unavailable.
gaps: Earlier development history cannot be recovered from this repository.

questionsAnswered.question: If the proposed change adds, removes, renames, retypes, or reinterprets the value, what is directly affected?
questionsAnswered.answer: The implemented reinterpretation directly affects parsing, layout, positions, drag dispatch, canvas entities/edges, relation colors, legend, and fallback selection.

questionsAnswered.question: What is transitively affected by the proposed change?
questionsAnswered.answer: Zoom, fit, pan, scroll region, status help, README, renderer use, and error behavior are transitively affected.

questionsAnswered.question: Could existing stored data, API clients, event consumers, exhaustive branches, reports, or UI code reject or misunderstand the new behavior?
questionsAnswered.answer: No stored data or external clients exist. Unsupported or partially supported Mermaid syntax is routed to the existing static renderer to prevent misinterpretation.

questionsAnswered.question: Are a migration, backfill, compatibility period, contract version, feature flag, rollout plan, or rollback plan required?
questionsAnswered.answer: No migration or feature flag is needed. Automatic fallback is the compatibility mechanism; rollout is the desktop update; rollback reverts c57f240.

questionsAnswered.question: Which conclusions are confirmed by static analysis, runtime observation, contracts, declarations, or Git history?
questionsAnswered.answer: Declarations and flows are confirmed by static evidence, expected parser behavior by test definitions, renderer dependency by contract, and changes by Git history; runtime GUI evidence is unavailable.
gaps: Tests are authored but not runtime-confirmed.

questionsAnswered.question: Which relationships remain inferred, dynamic, external, or unknown?
questionsAnswered.answer: Runtime drag ergonomics, Tk rendering on the user machine, broad Mermaid syntax coverage, more-than-eight-label color reuse, and external mmdc reliability remain dynamic or unverified.
gaps: Python/Tk runtime is unavailable in this execution environment.

unresolvedDynamicCalls: Tk GUI event dispatch was not runtime-observed.
Mermaid CLI fallback behavior remains external.
Unsupported Mermaid flowchart syntax intentionally routes to static fallback.

gaps: Python is not installed in the execution environment, so unit and GUI tests could not run.
Dragged positions are session-only and reset on reload.
Palette colors repeat after eight unique relationship labels.
recommendedVerification: Install/use Python 3.10+ and run py -m unittest -v.
Open sample.mmd and drag every entity.
Confirm connected edges and labels move with the entity.
Confirm every legend swatch matches its edge and label.
Exercise wheel zoom, background pan, fit, reload, and an unsupported diagram fallback.
Parse JSON and validate every edge endpoint.
